source.title: Uniform commercial code:analysis of effect on existing Kentucky law /[By the Legislative Research Commission, in cooperation with the Kentucky State Bar Association's Committee on the Uniform Commercial Code]
source.author: Kentucky. General Assembly

pages.text:
and bill of exchange have been sent, without express instructions, may properly surrender the bill of lading on acceptance (without payment) of the bill of exchange. See 2 Williston, *Sales*, Sec. 290. *National Bank of Commerce v. Merchants' National Bank*, 91 U.S. 92 (1875); *Moore v. Louisiana Nat. Bank*, 44 La. 99, 10 So. 407 (1892). If the bill of exchange which goes forward with the bill of lading is payable on demand, it is equally clear that the bill of lading should not be surrendered until the bill of exchange is paid, not merely accepted; acceptance of a demand bill is not customary. 2 Williston, *Sales*, Sec. 290; *Tampico Banking Co., S.A. v. Barber*, 3 F. 2d 136 (5th Cir. 1925) (involved in a sight draft). Also it is well established that if a bank, receiving for collection a draft with a bill of lading attached, under instructions to deliver the latter only upon payment of the draft, surrenders the bill of lading without collecting the draft, it is liable for the loss sustained by reason of such unauthorized action. *Shippee v. Pallotti, Andretta & Co.*, 117 Conn. 472, 168 Atl. 880 (1933).

Attention is directed to the fact that while the cases cited are limited to bills of lading this section is broader, being designed to cover any document against which a draft may be drawn whatever may be its form. See also UCC 3-703 and 5-112.

---

**Section 2-515. Preserving Evidence of Goods in Dispute.**

**Summary**

In furtherance of the adjustment of any claim or dispute:

(a) either party on notice to the other and for the purpose of ascertaining facts and preserving evidence may inspect, test and sample the goods including those in the possession or control of the other; and

(b) the parties may agree to a third party inspection or survey to determine the conformity or condition of the goods and may agree that the findings shall be binding upon them in any subsequent litigation or adjustment.

**Kentucky Statutes**

None.

**Comment**

This section is new to the sales law, no corresponding provision appearing in the Uniform Sales Act, KRS Ch. 361. It represents another instance of integrating the rules of evidence and practice and procedure into the Code as is pointed up in the provision which entitles either party, upon notice, to an inspection or testing of the goods in the possession of the other party to the dispute, for the purpose of establishing evidence in the dispute.

Subsection (b) conforms to the existing favorable attitude toward the settlement of disputes by arbitrators. See KRS Ch. 417.

---

**Section 5-102. Scope.**

**Summary**

(1) This article applies:
    (a) to a credit issued by a bank if the credit requires a documentary draft or a documentary demand for payment; and
    (b) to a credit issued by a person other than a bank if the credit requires that the draft or demand for payment be accompanied by a document of title; and
    (c) to a credit issued by a bank or other person if the credit is not within subparagraphs (a) or (b) but conspicuously states that it is a letter of credit or is conspicuously so entitled.

(2) Unless the engagement meets the requirements of subsection (1), this article does not apply to engagements to make advances or to honor drafts or demands for payment, to authorities to pay or purchase, to guarantees or to general agreements.

(3) This article deals with some but not all of the rules and concepts of letters of credit as such rules or concepts have developed prior to this act or may hereafter develop. The fact that this article states a rule does not by itself require, imply or negate application of the same or a converse rule to a situation not provided for or to a person not specified by this article.

**Kentucky Statutes**

None.

**Comment**

In its simplest form a letter of credit is an engagement by the issuer (bank), made at the request of the customer (buyer), to honor the beneficiary's (seller's) drafts. Subsection (1) states precisely the scope of Article 5. All bank letters of credit of a documentary nature are governed by the article. So far as credits issued by persons other than a bank are concerned, only a credit which is in the traditional form, requiring a draft or demand for payment accompanied by documents of title, falls mandatorily within the scope of the article. Clean credits — those requiring presentation of drafts only, without documents — are included if the document clearly indicates that it is a letter of credit.

Banking practice with respect to letters of credit, not only in Kentucky but also throughout the country and to a large extent internationally, has tended to become unified because of the adherence by banks to a set of rules promulgated and revised from time to time by the International Chamber of Commerce. The current regulation, effective since January 1, 1952, is known as the "Uniform Customs and Practice of Commercial Documentary Credits Fixed by the Thirteenth Congress of the International Chamber of Commerce." Originally adopted in 1938, the Uniform Customs are effective in controlling relations between banks which have agreed

# Kentucky Statutes

## KRS 426.005.

### Comment

Subsection (1) gives a secured party the rights provided under Part 5 and also the right to reduce his claim to judgment where the debtor defaults. KRS 426.005 presently provides that judgment may be rendered in an action to enforce a mortgage or lien.

Subsection (2) is a cross-reference to UCC 9-207 as to the rights and duties to which the secured party is subject when in possession of the collateral.

Subsection (3) does not permit a waiver or variation of rights and duties but permits the parties to agree as to standards by which the fulfillment of the rights and duties is to be measured if the standard is not manifestly unreasonable.

Subsection (4) permits proceedings under this part as to the personalty if the agreement covers both real and personal property, but if the secured party proceeds under the real estate mortgage law, as to both, this part does not apply. Under present Kentucky law, a secured party may proceed as to both real and personal property under KRS 426.005, 426.130 and 426.140 which require that personal property be sold first, and if it is not sufficient to satisfy the judgment, then real property may be sold and the proceeds used to satisfy the judgment.

Subsection (5) makes it clear that a judicial sale on a judgment is a foreclosure of a security interest to which the requirements of Part 5 do not apply. Kentucky seems to be in accord, but KRS 426.200 and 426.530 give the debtor a redemptive right for one year after the sale where the collateral is land and does not sell for two-thirds of its appraisal value. KRS 426.525 provides that foreclosure of a mortgage is forbidden. But *Montenegro-Riehm Music Co. v. Beuris*, 160 Ky. 557, 196 S. W. 986 (1914) held that a sale of a mortgaged chattel by the mortgagee under a power of sale was a mere enforcing of the mortgagee's lien and did not violate the section. The court also held that the mortgagor's right of redemption was barred forever, and that the title to the chattel forfeited without a sale. See also *Farmers and Deposior's Bank v. Taylor*, 290 Ky. 774, 162 S. W. 2d 764 (1942).

---

## Section 9-502. Collection Rights of Secured Party.

### Summary

(1) When so agreed and in any event on default the secured party is entitled to notify an account debtor or the obligor on an instrument to make

386

v. Southwest Cracker Co., 225 Ky. 418, 9 S. W. 2d 119 (1928). A collateral question is whether buyer, although accepting, is barred from claim for damage by delay in giving notice. See UCC 2-607, *infra*.

(2) *Acceptance of Part of Commercial Unit*. The Uniform Sales Act has no provision comparable to subsection (2). But cases on partial rejection (See UCC 2-601, *supra*) refer to the test of "divisibility" defined in Sec. 76 of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.760. See *Blue Grass Tract Co. v. Hedges*, 139 Ky. 358, 104 S. W. 370 (1907).

---

**Section 2-607. Effect of Acceptance; Notice of Breach; Burden of Establishing Breach After Acceptance; Notice of Claim or Litigation to Person Answerable Over.**

**Summary**

(1) The buyer must pay at the contract rate for any goods accepted.

(2) Acceptance of goods precludes their rejection and if made with knowledge of nonconformity cannot be revoked unless the acceptance was made on the reasonable assumption the nonconformity would be cured, but acceptance does not impair any other remedy for nonconformity.

(3) Where a tender has been accepted:
    (a) the buyer must within a reasonable time after he discovers or should have discovered any breach notify the seller or be barred from any remedy; and
    (b) if claim is for infringement or the like (subsection (3) of UCC 2-312) and buyer is sued as result of such breach he must notify the seller of the litigation within a reasonable time after he receives notice of the litigation or be barred from any remedy over for liability established by the litigation.

(4) Burden is on buyer to establish any breach with respect to the goods accepted.

(5) Where buyer receives notice that he is being sued for breach of warranty or other obligation for which his seller is answerable over:
    (a) he may give his seller written notice of the litigation. If notice states that seller may come in and defend and that if seller does not do so he will be bound in any action against him by his buyer by any determination of fact common to the two litigations, then, unless seller after seasonable receipt of notice does come in and defend he is so bound.
    (b) if the claim is for infringement or the like (subsection (3) of UCC 2-312) the original seller may demand in writing that his buyer turn over to him control of the litigation including settlement or else be barred from any remedy over and if he

92
over a subsequent purchaser even though the later purchased draft or demand has been first honored.

## Kentucky Statutes

None.

### Comment

This section defines a notation credit and states the rules which are applicable to notation and other credits in the event of exhaustion of credit.

---

## Section 5-109. Issuer's Obligation to Its Customers.

### Summary

(1) An issuer's obligation to its customer includes good faith and observance of any general banking usage but unless otherwise agreed does not include liability or responsibility:

(a) for performance of the underlying contract for sale or other transaction between the customer and the beneficiary; or

(b) for any act or omission of any person other than itself or its own branch or for loss or destruction of a draft, demand or document in transit or in the possession of others; or

(c) based on knowledge or lack of knowledge of any usage of any particular trade.

(2) An issuer must examine documents with care so as to ascertain that on their face they appear to comply with the terms of the credit but unless otherwise agreed assumes no liability or responsibility for the genuineness, falsification or effect of any document which appears on such examination to be regular on its face.

(3) A non-bank issuer is not bound by any banking usage of which it has no knowledge.

## Kentucky Statutes

None.

### Comment

(1) Subsection (1) relieves the issuing bank of any responsibility for acts of its correspondents. This release of liability is frequently effected by the customer designating all parties involved, other than the issuer, as his agents rather than as agents of the issuer. Letters issued subject to the Uniform Customs might automatically incorporate similar releases found in Articles 11, 12, 13 and 14.

(2) Subsection (2) restates the rule of Article 9 of the Uniform Customs: "Banks must examine all documents and papers with care so as to ascertain that on their face they appear to be in order"; and the rule of Article 11 of the Uniform Customs:

242
KRS 426.220 and 426.530 gives a debtor a right of redemption in real property where it does not bring two-thirds of its appraised value at a sale in pursuance of a judgment or order of a court. This right must be exercised within one year after the sale.

---

**Section 9-507. Secured Party's Liability for Failure to Comply With This Part.**

**Summary**

(1) If it is established that the secured party is not proceeding in accordance with the provisions of this part disposition may be ordered or restrained on appropriate terms and conditions. If the disposition has occurred the debtor or any person entitled to notification or whose security interest has been made known to the secured party prior to the disposition has a right to recover from the secured party any loss caused by a failure to comply with the provisions of this part. If the collateral is consumer goods, the debtor has a right to recover in any event an amount not less than the credit service charge plus 10 percent of the principal amount of the debt or the time price differential plus 10 percent of the cash price.

(2) The fact that a better price could have been obtained by a sale at a different time or in a different method from that selected by the secured party is not of itself sufficient to establish that the sale was not made in a commercially reasonable manner. If the secured party either sells the collateral in the usual manner in any recognized market therefor or if he sells at the price current in such market at the time of his sale or if he has otherwise sold in conformity with reasonable commercial practices among dealers in the type of property sold he has sold in a commercially reasonable manner. The principles stated in the two preceding sentences with respect to sales also apply as may be appropriate to other types of disposition. A disposition which has been approved in any judicial proceeding or by any *bona fide* Creditor's Committee or representative of creditors shall conclusively be deemed to be commercially reasonable, but this sentence does not indicate that any such approval must be obtained in any case nor does it indicate that any disposition not so approved is commercially reasonable.

**Kentucky Statutes**

None.

**Comment**

The liability of the secured party for improper disposition is recognized in the present law. *Montenegro-Riehm Music Co. v. Beuris*, 160 Ky. 557, 169 S. W. 986 (1914).

Possibly the most important feature of subsection (1) is the right of the debtor to force or restrain disposition.

Subsection (2) is essentially a definition of "commercially reasonable" which together with "good faith" (see UCC 1-203) are the criteria for acts under the Commercial Code.

392
